text.title: AI PM Recipes Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every recipe MUST satisfy. Hard rules; an LLM-driven PM integration that violates them is a liability.

rule.statement: Every recipe uses TOKENS SCOPED to the minimum needed: Jira read-only on the relevant project keys, GitHub read on the relevant repos, Slack post-only on the digest channel. Admin-scoped tokens are FORBIDDEN. Tokens are stored in a secrets manager / env vars — NEVER committed.
rule.rationale: A compromised PM recipe with admin scope is a project-wide incident; with read-only scope on specific resources the blast radius is bounded. Routine misuse is the dominant pattern; scope discipline is the cheapest defence.

rule.statement: Every LLM call in every recipe MUST use structured output (Anthropic JSON mode, OpenAI structured output, or per-call JSON schema validation post-hoc). Free-text LLM output piped into downstream systems is FORBIDDEN. Output validates against the schema before any Slack post / Jira update / risk register write.

rationale.source: Faion product policy `feedback_agent_output`; LLM-output-driven integration failures

rule.rationale: Free-text output regularly contains malformed JSON, Markdown that breaks Slack parsing, or hallucinated entity names. Schema validation is the cheapest gate; recipes that skip it produce surprise downstream failures.

rule.statement: The Jira-digest recipe MUST cap output at 800 words and 8 risk callouts per run. Larger digests train the team to skim. Per-run cost MUST be capped (default $1 / run); exceeding the cap halts the recipe with an alert rather than burning quota silently.

rationale.source: Slack signal-vs-noise studies; LLM cost-control patterns

rule.rationale: A 4,000-word digest is read by no one. A 600-word digest with eight clearly-labelled risk callouts changes behaviour. The cost cap protects against prompt-explosion or upstream data spikes that would otherwise create a surprise bill.

rule.statement: The Loom-retro recipe outputs MUST follow the four-section template: what_worked, what_didnt, actions, blockers. Output without all four sections is REJECTED. Each action MUST have an owner + due date inferred from transcript context OR marked `owner_TBD`.

rationale.source: Retrospective best practice (Norm Kerth) + structured-output discipline

rule.rationale: Free-form retro notes lose action items; structured retro notes produce a tracked work queue. The four sections cover the canonical retro questions and force the LLM to attribute statements to one of them.

rule.statement: Recipes that MUTATE remote state (post to Slack, update Jira fields, comment on a PR) MUST include a `dry_run` flag and a `pm_signoff` flag. Default is dry_run=true. Flipping to live mode requires PM-recorded signoff. Auto-mutation without recorded signoff is FORBIDDEN.
rule.rationale: PM-tool mutations affect humans (team members, stakeholders). The dry-run gate keeps the recipe testable; the signoff gate makes the PM accountable for live behaviour.

example.title: Dry-run digest with structured output
example.code: # recipe-1-jira-digest.py (key fragment) DRY_RUN = os.environ.get("DRY_RUN", "true").lower() == "true" client = anthropic.Anthropic() resp = client.messages.create( model="claude-sonnet-4-7-20260120", max_tokens=1500, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}], response_format={"type": "json_schema", "schema": load_schema("digest-schema.json")}, ) digest = json.loads(resp.content[0].text) validate(digest, load_schema("digest-schema.json")) if not DRY_RUN and PM_SIGNOFF: slack_post(channel=DIGEST_CHANNEL, text=render(digest)) else: print(render(digest)) # dev review
example.note: r1 (scoped key in env), r2 (structured output + validate), r5 (dry_run + signoff) satisfied. Mutating step gated.

example.title: Free-text digest piped to Slack
example.note: r2, r3, r5 violations. No structured output, no cap, no dry-run, posted to all-hands. Classic "looked great in dev, scary in prod" failure.
